sections.title: Plans & Intentions: -(으)ㄹ 거예요
sections.explanation: The most common way to express future plans and intentions in Korean is -(으)ㄹ 거예요. This roughly translates to 'I am going to...' or 'I will...' when talking about personal plans. Formation: if the verb stem ends in a vowel, add -ㄹ 거예요; if it ends in a consonant, add -을 거예요. If the stem ends in ㄹ, it already has the ㄹ so just add 거예요. Examples: 가다 → 갈 거예요 (will go), 먹다 → 먹을 거예요 (will eat), 만들다 → 만들 거예요 (will make), 하다 → 할 거예요 (will do). This form can also express a guess or expectation about a future situation: 내일 비가 올 거예요 (It will probably rain tomorrow). It is the most versatile and frequently used future form in everyday Korean.
sections.tip: Vowel stem → -ㄹ 거예요 (가 → 갈 거예요). Consonant stem → -을 거예요 (먹 → 먹을 거예요). ㄹ stem → just add 거예요 (만들 → 만들 거예요). Think of 거 as short for 것 (thing) — literally 'it is a thing I will do.'

examples.en: I will go to school tomorrow.
What will you eat for dinner?
I am going to watch a movie on the weekend.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I will study' using 공부하다?
exercises.explanation: 공부하다 → stem 하 (ends in vowel) → add -ㄹ 거예요: 공부할 거예요. Note the space between 할 and 거예요.

exercises.prompt: Conjugate 읽다 (to read) to the future tense.
exercises.explanation: 읽다 → stem 읽 (ends in consonant) → add -을 거예요: 읽을 거예요 (will read).

exercises.prompt: What does 비가 올 거예요 mean?
exercises.explanation: -(으)ㄹ 거예요 can express both personal plans and predictions/expectations. 비가 올 거예요 means 'It will probably rain' — a prediction about the future.

sections.title: Conjecture & Strong Will: -겠-
sections.explanation: The suffix -겠- has two main uses: expressing the speaker's strong will or determination, and making conjectures or guesses. For will/determination (first person): 제가 하겠습니다 (I will do it — with determination), 열심히 공부하겠어요 (I will study hard — a firm commitment). For conjecture (about others or situations): 맛있겠다! (That must be delicious!), 힘들겠어요 (That must be tough), 내일 춥겠어요 (It will probably be cold tomorrow). The formality marker -겠- is often heard in formal settings: 알겠습니다 (I understand — literally 'I will know'), 모르겠어요 (I don't know / I'm not sure — literally 'I won't know'). The difference from -(으)ㄹ 거예요: -겠- implies stronger personal resolve or a subjective guess, while -(으)ㄹ 거예요 is for general plans and more objective predictions.
sections.tip: -겠- is easy to form: just insert 겠 between the stem and the ending. 가다 → 가겠어요, 먹다 → 먹겠어요. No vowel harmony rules — 겠 is the same for all verbs. Remember: first person = determination, second/third person = conjecture.

examples.en: I will do it. (strong determination, formal)
I understand. (lit. I will know — very common formal phrase)

options: You must be tired. (conjecture about someone)
exercises.explanation: When -겠- is used about someone else, it expresses conjecture or empathy: 피곤하겠어요 means 'You must be tired' or 'That must be tiring.' It is not about the speaker's intention.

options: -겠- expresses stronger will or subjective guesses; -(으)ㄹ 거예요 is for general plans
exercises.explanation: -겠- conveys personal determination or subjective conjecture, while -(으)ㄹ 거예요 is the neutral, everyday future for plans and predictions.

exercises.prompt: What does 모르겠어요 mean in everyday speech?
options: I don't know / I'm not sure
I will not know in the future
exercises.explanation: 모르겠어요 literally means 'I won't/can't know,' but in practice it is the standard way to say 'I don't know' or 'I'm not sure.' It is softer and more polite than 몰라요.

sections.title: Negating the Future
sections.explanation: Negating future tense works with the same two patterns. With 안: place 안 before the verb in its future form. 안 갈 거예요 (will not go), 안 먹을 거예요 (will not eat). For 하다 verbs: 공부 안 할 거예요 (will not study). With -지 않다: attach -지 않다 to the stem, then conjugate 않다 to the future: 가지 않을 거예요 (will not go), 먹지 않을 거예요 (will not eat). For -겠-: 안 가겠어요 or 가지 않겠어요. There is also a special negative future form -(으)ㄹ 리가 없다 for expressing disbelief: 그럴 리가 없어요 (There is no way that is true). In casual conversation, 안 + future is the most common pattern.
sections.tip: Negation with the future works exactly like present and past: 안 before the verb, or -지 않다 on the stem. The future ending goes on the main verb (or on 않다 in the long form).

examples.en: I will not eat. (-지 않다 form)

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I will not buy it' using 안 and 사다 (to buy)?
exercises.explanation: Place 안 before the future form: 안 살 거예요. 사다 → stem 사 → future modifier 살 → 안 살 거예요 (will not buy).

exercises.prompt: Negate 할 거예요 (will do) using the -지 않다 form.
exercises.explanation: Stem 하 + 지 않다, then conjugate 않다 to future: 하지 않을 거예요 (will not do). The future marker goes on 않다, not on 하다.

exercises.prompt: Which is the correct negation of 공부하다 using 안 in the future?
exercises.explanation: For 하다 verbs, 안 goes between the noun and 할 거예요: 공부 안 할 거예요, just like in present and past tense.
